div.div: pnpm still has to download the tarball from the registry (npm registry doesn't let you fetch individual files from a package). It always downloads and unpacks the full tarball into memory/temp. But when it's time to write files into the store / link them into node_modules, that's where the dedup kicks in — unchanged files skip the write-to-disk step and just get relinked, and the actual node_modules population is hardlinks either way, not copies. So the savings are mainly in disk space and node_modules population speed, not network bandwidth (there are some registry-level tricks for reducing download size too, but that's separate from the store's content-addressing).

div.p: so u r saying pnpm does not only calculate the hash of the each pacakges, instead it calculates teh hash of each and every file in that packages, so that later on when we download the different version of the same package, so instead of downloading the full packages with full files it will only download the files from that package those hash is not present and for the hash file it will just link those files to that newer version of that package

box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;

li.p: Downloads the full tarball: It pulls the complete .tgz file for the new version from the npm registry.
Extracts and hashes: It unpacks the tarball in a temporary location and calculates the hash of every single file.
Compares to the global store: It checks if those file hashes already exist in your local pnpm store.
Saves only the diff: It takes any new files (hashes it hasn't seen before) and saves them to the global store. It discards the downloaded files that it already has.
Hard-links to node_modules: Finally, it creates hard links in your project folder, pointing to the newly added files and the previously cached files.

switch.text: Downloads the full tarball: It pulls the complete .tgz file for the new version from the npm registry...

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 658px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1705px; margin-left: 376px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: "Helvetica"; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.li: pnpm fetches the new version's metadata/tarball.
For each file in that tarball, it computes the content hash.

It checks: does a file with this exact hash already exist in the store?
Yes → skip writing it, just reuse the existing stored file (hardlink it into node_modules when needed).
No → write this new file into the store under its new hash.

So if you go from lodash@4.17.20 → 4.17.21 and only 3 files changed internally, only those 3 files' new hashes get added to the store. Every unchanged file (which is often the vast majority — license files, unchanged utility files, type defs, etc.) is instantly deduplicated against what's already sitting in the store from the old version, or even from a completely different package that happens to ship an identical file.

font.style: color: light-dark(rgb(250, 250, 250), rgb(255, 255, 255)); background-color: light-dark(rgb(22, 22, 22), rgb(0, 0, 153));

p.font: Avoids huge flat directories — A single project can have tens of thousands of files; putting all blobs in one dir would make filesystem lookups slow (ext4/NTFS do linear or hashed scans of directory entries).

li.style: box-sizing: border-box; border: 0px solid light-dark(currentcolor, rgb(237, 237, 237)); margin: 0px 0px 8px; padding: 0px; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; text-rendering: geometricprecision;

p.style: box-sizing: border-box; border: 0px solid light-dark(currentcolor, rgb(237, 237, 237)); margin: 0px; padding: 0px; display: inline; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; text-rendering: geometricprecision;

font.style: color: light-dark(rgb(250, 250, 250), rgb(255, 255, 255)); background-color: light-dark(rgb(22, 22, 22), rgb(0, 0, 153));

p.font: Even distribution — The hash is uniform, so the first byte distributes files roughly evenly across 256 buckets.

li.style: box-sizing: border-box; border: 0px solid light-dark(currentcolor, rgb(237, 237, 237)); margin: 0px 0px 8px; padding: 0px; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; text-rendering: geometricprecision;

p.style: box-sizing: border-box; border: 0px solid light-dark(currentcolor, rgb(237, 237, 237)); margin: 0px; padding: 0px; display: inline; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; text-rendering: geometricprecision;

font.style: color: light-dark(rgb(250, 250, 250), rgb(255, 255, 255)); background-color: light-dark(rgb(22, 22, 22), rgb(0, 0, 153));

p.font: File path layout: A blob is stored at files/<first-2-hex-chars>/<full-hash>. For example: files/3f/3f9a1b2c.... The first byte determines the shard directory.

const hash = "00cc06fe80fe136b07a2bcc082944..."; // just computed from file bytes

const fileName = hash.slice(2); // rest of hash

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

li.code: Inside every project's node_modules, pnpm creates a hidden folder: node_modules/.pnpm/. This is where the actual package contents live — one subfolder per exact package version:

code.class: bg-text-200/5 border border-0.5 border-border-300 text-danger-000 whitespace-pre-wrap rounded-[0.4rem] px-1 py-px text-[0.9rem]

li.code: For each package version, pnpm reads that package's index file and, for every entry, hardlinks the corresponding content-hash file from store/v11/files/xx/... into node_modules/.pnpm/lodash@4.17.21/node_modules/lodash/index.js (etc.), reconstructing the original folder structure of the package using its recorded relative paths, even though the store itself has no folder structure.
li.span: Repeat for every file in the package. Now you have a complete, real lodash package folder — just made of hardlinks instead of copies.

ol.li: pnpm links top-level dependencies via symlinks: - Now your project's actual node_modules/lodash (the one your code does require('lodash') or import against) is not a real folder — it's a symlink pointing into .pnpm node_modules/lodash → .pnpm/lodash@4.17.21/node_modules/lodash

nested/transitive dependencies link to each other inside .pnpm: - If lodash itself depended on some other package, that dependency would be symlinked inside .pnpm/lodash@4.17.21/node_modules/, pointing to its own version folder elsewhere in .pnpm.

div.div:
Older versions of npm (before npm v3) nested dependencies deeply, but npm v3+ and Yarn Classic started flattening dependencies whenever possible.
You never owned this dependency.

div.div:
npm before v3 (nested dependencies): - Before npm v3, dependencies were nested exactly according to the dependency graph.
Problems with npm v2
Lots of duplicate packages.
Deeply nested node_modules.
Windows often hit the MAX_PATH limit because paths became extremely long.

div.div:
npm v3+ (flattened): -
Starting with npm v3, npm tried to hoist as many dependencies as possible to the top level.
This solved the deep nesting problem, but introduced the phantom dependency problem because your app could now accidentally access picocolors.
